diabetic retinopathy: The fundus image shows multiple microaneurysms, which appear as small, distinct red dots in the retina.
A key feature is the presence of dot and blot hemorrhages, indicating bleeding within the deeper layers of the retina.
This photo displays hard exudates, which are well-defined, yellowish deposits of lipids and proteins leaking from damaged vessels.
Visible are cotton wool spots, which are fluffy, white, superficial patches caused by nerve fiber layer ischemia.
The image reveals venous beading, a characteristic finding where retinal veins show localized areas of constriction and dilation, resembling beads on a string.
This is an image showing intraretinal microvascular abnormalities (IRMA), which are shunt vessels that bypass the capillary network.
Neovascularization is evident on the optic disc (NVD), appearing as a fine network of new, fragile blood vessels.
The photo shows neovascularization elsewhere (NVE) in the retina, away from the optic disc.
A key sign of proliferative diabetic retinopathy is a vitreous hemorrhage, which can obscure the view of the retina.
This image displays a tractional retinal detachment, where fibrovascular tissue contracts and pulls the retina away from its underlying layer.
The macula appears thickened and edematous, consistent with diabetic macular edema.
Retinal blood vessels are tortuous and dilated, a general sign of diabetic vasculopathy.
This photo shows laser photocoagulation scars, appearing as small, atrophic, pale spots from previous treatment.
In this image, the background retinopathy is mild, with only a few microaneurysms visible.
This is a photo of severe non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR), characterized by the 4-2-1 rule: hemorrhages in 4 quadrants, venous beading in 2, or IRMA in 1.
glaucoma: The image shows an abnormally large optic cup-to-disc ratio, indicating loss of neural tissue.
A key feature is the thinning of the neuroretinal rim, especially in the inferior and superior regions, violating the 'ISNT' rule.
This photo displays notching of the neuroretinal rim, a focal area where the rim has been completely lost.
Visible in the image is a disc hemorrhage (Drance hemorrhage), a small flame-shaped bleed on or near the optic disc margin.
The lamina cribrosa, the sieve-like structure at the base of the cup, is more visible, a sign of advanced cupping.
This is an image showing 'bayoneting' of the blood vessels, where vessels take a sharp, deep bend as they cross the excavated rim.
The photo reveals a nerve fiber layer defect, which appears as a dark, wedge-shaped area radiating from the optic disc.
Asymmetry of the cup-to-disc ratio of more than 0.2 between the two eyes is a significant finding.
This image shows 'saucerization' of the optic cup, a shallow, sloping excavation of the entire disc.
The neuroretinal rim appears pale, indicating atrophy of the nerve tissue.
This photo shows peripapillary atrophy, a zone of depigmentation and choroidal thinning around the optic disc.
The optic disc appears vertically elongated, a common finding in glaucomatous cupping.
In this image, the central retinal vessel trunk is displaced nasally due to temporal rim loss.
This is a fundus photo of an eye with advanced glaucoma, showing 'bean-pot' cupping, where the cup is deeply excavated.
The overall appearance is of a 'hollowed-out' optic nerve head, consistent with glaucomatous optic neuropathy.
cataract: The image shows a generalized opacification of the crystalline lens, obscuring the view of the retina.
A key feature is the diminished or absent red reflex when light is shone into the eye.
This photo, taken with a slit lamp, reveals a dense nuclear sclerotic cataract, with a yellowish or brownish hue in the center of the lens.
Visible are cortical spokes, which are radial, wedge-shaped opacities extending from the periphery of the lens inward.
The image displays a posterior subcapsular cataract, appearing as a plaque-like, granular opacity on the posterior surface of the lens.
This is an image of a mature cataract, where the entire lens is opaque and white, and the iris may cast a shadow on it.
The fundus details, such as the optic disc and retinal vessels, are blurred and difficult to visualize through the cloudy lens.
This photo shows a congenital cataract, a lens opacity present at birth, which may appear as a white spot in the pupil.
In this retroillumination image, the cataractous changes appear as dark defects against the bright, reflected red reflex.
This is an image of a traumatic cataract, often characterized by a star-shaped or 'rosette' pattern of opacity.
The anterior chamber appears shallow, a finding that can be associated with an advanced, swollen (intumescent) cataract.
Visible are Morgagnian cataract features, where the cortex has liquefied, allowing the dense nucleus to sink inferiorly.
This photo shows 'phacomorphic' changes, where the swollen cataractous lens pushes the iris forward.
The overall image clarity is significantly reduced, as if viewing the world through a foggy window.
This is a slit-lamp view showing vacuoles and water clefts within the lens cortex, early signs of cataract formation.
normal retina: The fundus image displays a sharply demarcated, round or vertically oval optic disc.
A key feature is a healthy, pinkish-orange neuroretinal rim of uniform thickness, following the 'ISNT' rule.
This photo shows a normal optic cup-to-disc ratio, typically less than 0.5.
Visible in the image is a bright foveal reflex, a pinpoint reflection of light from the center of the macula.
The macula appears as a darker, avascular area temporal to the optic disc, without any pigmentary changes or edema.
This is an image showing normal retinal vasculature, with arteries appearing lighter and thinner than the darker, wider veins.
The normal artery-to-vein caliber ratio is approximately 2-to-3.
This photo shows the retinal vessels emerging from the optic disc and branching smoothly across the retina.
The background fundus has a uniform, reddish-orange color, reflecting a healthy retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and choroid.
There is a complete absence of hemorrhages, exudates, cotton wool spots, or microaneurysms.
This image shows a clear vitreous, with no signs of floaters, haze, or hemorrhage.
The retinal periphery is flat and attached, with no tears, holes, or detachments.
This is a photo of a normal retina, characterized by the absence of any drusen, neovascularization, or abnormal pigmentation.
The nerve fiber layer is clearly visible as fine striations radiating from the optic disc.
The overall appearance is of a healthy posterior segment of the eye, with all anatomical structures appearing unremarkable.
